data.title: Pfizer Could Surprise Again This Earnings Season (Preview)
data.description:
Previewing Pfizer's Upcoming Q1 Earnings Release

My previous update on Pfizer (PFE) was essentially a preview article for the firm's then-upcoming Q4 release in February 2025. I kept my "Buy" rating in place, and although the stock opened with a huge gap down when the Q4 data came out (almost 5% below its previous close), Pfizer quickly recovered and is now trading 2-3% above its pre-Q4 price.

The company releases its Q1 earnings on May 5th, in less than 2 weeks, so I think it's time for me to update my reasoning and take a look at what's coming next for PFE.

First off, let me briefly refresh your memories regarding the Q4 results. For the full year 2025, Pfizer had ~$62.6 billion in consolidated revenues (-2% YoY), which looks like a clear stagnation at first glance, but if we exclude the impact of Comirnaty and Paxlovid (COVID drugs), the firm's top line would have been up by 6%, which is actually pretty solid given the entity's size. The non-COVID drug portfolio is gaining traction right now.

In Q4 in particular, we've seen $17.56 billion in revenues (-1.1% YoY) and an adjusted EPS of $0.66 (+4.76% YoY). Both numbers beat the consensus estimates by 4.33% and 16.18%, respectively, according to Seeking Alpha. What I find interesting here is that the revenue decline started to improve when we compare the Q4 results with the full-year dynamics, and at the very same time, the adjusted EPS even went up (albeit by less than 5%), which is already a great start to the turnaround. The market and Wall Street analysts in particular still refuse to see that turnaround in PFE's profitability - the firm has been beating the consensus estimates multiple consecutive quarters, with an average beating size of over 20-30%:

Lower-margin pandemic-era drugs are clearly fading away from the revenue mix, and that's why Pfizer's gross margins have risen significantly (from 49.25% in 2023 to 76% as of 2025). The OPEX is also getting better thanks to the cost control/cutting programs that I discussed in my previous articles on PFE. What we need to see next is management's execution on the commercialization of the existing product pipeline - the top line should start moving in the right direction after a prolonged period of stagnation to allow the market to finally reprice PFE to the upside.

Pfizer has been trying to reshape the duopoly-like obesity market, where Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) have their well-known selling products and heavily monetize them. To rival them, Pfizer bought Metsera for $7 billion, now owning a highly differentiated pipeline of clinical-stage injectable and oral obesity candidates - the story should eventually turn bullish for Pfizer this year, in my opinion, as Pfizer initiates 10 Phase 3 trials for PF'3944 (formerly MET-097i), an ultra-long-acting GLP-1 receptor agonist that promises true once-monthly dosing.

The obesity market is big enough for all three companies to enjoy elevated growth rates in the next few years, but beyond obesity, Pfizer has a highly productive 2026-2027 period for its pipeline, having 20 key pivotal Phase 3 studies.

Pfizer plans to initiate/advance 20 pivotal studies in 2026, which include 10 pivotal studies for the ultra-long-acting obesity candidates added from the Metsera acquisition and four for PF-08634404, a dual PD-1/VEGF inhibitor in-licensed from Chinese biotech 3SBio in 2025. Pfizer expects 2026 to be a catalyst-rich year, with some key data-readouts expected in the obesity and oncology programs.

Source

Among the most anticipated Phase 3 data readouts, Pfizer has sigvotatug vedotin (an antibody-drug conjugate for non-small cell lung cancer), Elrexfio (for double-class exposed relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma), and data for its Lyme disease vaccine candidate. If we quickly research the monetary potential for these end markets, we'll quickly realize that all of them are projected to reach tens of billions in TAM by 2030-35, which is giving Pfizer a pretty solid replacement base for the upcoming LOEs (if trials go smoothly and the firm starts to monetize shortly).

Another near-term bullish catalyst I see is a potential major regulatory win this year for Pfizer's Padcev (they got it following the Seagen acquisition). According to PFE's press release, the FDA has already granted Priority Review to Padcev (in combination with Keytruda) as a perioperative treatment for MIBC (muscle-invasive bladder cancer) regardless of cisplatin eligibility, with a target action date of August 17, 2026. So, if it gets approved, Pfizer will get a massive bullish revenue catalyst for 2027, having Padcev as the first and only perioperative treatment for this patient population.

We don't have to cancel out the ongoing cost-cutting, which is not going to stop - for 2026-2027, the management guided for a ramped-up enterprise-wide cost-saving initiatives with $7.7 billion in total savings through 2027, which is a lot for a firm that has annual OPEX at $56-57 billion. They first need to finish the "Realigning Our Cost Base" program by the end of 2026, and then work on the "Manufacturing Optimization Program" (additional $1.5 billion in net savings). Plus, they can save more from AI enablement for their R&D pipeline, and it's not currently priced in by the analysts, in my opinion.

Now, let's switch to the closest catalyst - the upcoming earnings release. For the full year 2026, the management team sees $59.5-62.5 billion with adjusted EPS of $2.80-3.00, incorporating $5 billion in expected COVID-19 product revenues plus ~$1.5 billion in revenue compression due to generic entries. The management guided for the full year, and I couldn't find the Q1 targets. The past beats show a near-perfect track record for the EPS (8 EPS beats and 0 misses over the past 2 years), while on the top line, we see 7 beats and only 1 miss over the same timeframe, which is also quite solid. Despite that, analysts are increasingly leaning bearish leading into the next few quarters, as we see massive 5-15% downward quarterly revisions over the past 6 months:

Pfizer's past beats were mainly supported by aggressive cost control. In the previous call, we've seen the management raising its overall cost savings target to a massive $7.7 billion through 2027, so I don't think Pfizer's margins will drop as harshly as it's currently priced in. In my opinion, Pfizer now has a much thicker margin cushion, so to speak, to drive its earnings beats even if top-line revenue simply meets expectations. That's why I expect the Q4-like beating for the upcoming quarter as well, and also some positive guidance updates around the non-COVID portfolio. They previously guided at ~4% YoY growth there (excluding LOE impacts), so if we hear that 4% again (or better 5-6%), that will become a clear trigger for repricing.

A few words about the valuation side. I know P/E is probably not everyone's best metric when it comes to valuing pharma giants like Pfizer, but I'll take it for simplicity and conventionalism. Right now, PFE is trading at 9.212x forward P/E, which is 2-3 times below the TTM averages of the past 5-10 years.

According to Seeking Alpha, compared with most stocks in the healthcare sector, PFE is priced at a 46-48% discount on forward P/E, depending on whether we use the non-GAAP or GAAP figures.

The consensus says PFE is going to experience a massive earnings contraction over the next few years, which is why the above discount is there:

We've seen constant beats in the past quarters, and if PFE manages to show just 1-2% YoY growth in EPS for 2026 with all the cost-cutting and new programs, a P/E range of 10-12x should be fair, considering the current pipeline and existing scale (even if LOE eats away at some critical drug sales in the next few years). So, assuming only 1.5% YoY rise for FY2026 EPS, and targeting 11x, I see a fair price per share of $35.95:

$3.22 * 1.015 * 11x = ~$35.95/share

Source: Oakoff's calculations

Today, one can buy PFE for $27.31 apiece, so based on my updated model, there's an upside potential of ~32%.

Risks

The patent cliff, as people call it, is the biggest risk that I see to any PFE bullish thesis. There are plenty of LOEs (loss of exclusivity) between 2026 and 2028, when the company is going to lose $15 billion to $18 billion in annual revenue, on average. Blockbusters like Eliquis (anticoagulant, $7.9B in 2025 sales), Ibrance (breast cancer, $4.1B), and Xtandi (prostate cancer, $2.1B) will all face generic competition around 2027, so it's not going to be a one-off risk-off for Pfizer, which is why the consensus paints EPS YoY losses up to the end of fiscal 2029. What I think the consensus is missing is the monetization potential of the current pipeline over the same time, but it's a different story - we're talking about potential here, that might not materialize, while LOEs are inevitable.

So, if the consensus is right and I'm wrong, then Pfizer might adjust to the downside from here as its valuation multiples keep compressing further. At 8x earnings and no adjustments made to the FY2026 EPS consensus, the fair value falls to $23.68/share, which is 13-14% below today's stock price.

Your Takeaway

Despite the clear LOE risks, to which we can add competition risks on their new end markets (mainly obesity), I think Pfizer looks solid ahead of its yet another earnings print. There's a strong track record of beating the consensus estimates, and seeing that there were no bullish revisions following Pfizer's bullish updates in Q4, I assume the history is very likely to repeat itself again. I have a price target of over $27/share, which leads PFE to an upside potential of about 30%. That's why I'm reiterating my previously assigned "Buy" rating today.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Can The Market Look Past Coming Headwinds?
data.description:
Yesterday, stocks finally gave back some gains over concerns that the deadline for the ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran would come and go today with no resolution. President Trump warned earlier in the day that there would be no extension and that bombing would resume with no deal. Vice President Vance was prepared to board a flight to Pakistan to engage in a second round of talks. At the last minute, his trip was cancelled because Iran had no intentions of showing up. Nor did the regime seem concerned about the end of the ceasefire. Unsurprisingly, after the market close, Trump extended the ceasefire indefinitely to allow mediators in Pakistan to encourage Iran to present a proposal to the Trump administration. Still, Iran has not responded on that front either, and overnight, the IRGC attacked two vessels for violating its blockade.

Iran refuses to engage until the President lifts the blockade on the Strait of Hormuz for its vessels, which he has refused to do. Therefore, oil prices remain elevated, with WTI climbing above $90, while Brent inches back towards $100. It is not just the supply of crude oil that is constrained, but also fertilizer, helium, and liquefied natural gas. Iran is playing the waiting game, as it knows the longer the strait remains closed and prices remain elevated, the more economic pressure it puts on the global economy and the Trump administration. The ball seems to be in President Trump’s court, but the clock is ticking.

Stocks rose after hours on news about the extension of the ceasefire, but that seems shortsighted to me, ignoring the coming headwinds that strengthen each day the waterway remains closed. Granted, retail sales soared in March by 1.7%, largely due to gasoline sales, but core sales were also impressive, increasing 0.6%. This strength in the face of rising gas prices had a lot to do with extra income from tax refunds, but that one-time payment has a temporary benefit.

The strong retail sales figure lifted the Atlanta Fed’s first-quarter economic growth estimate to 1.2%, but the primary engine of our economy, the consumer, has lost strength over the past three quarters, as consumer spending (PCE) contributed 2.3% to overall growth in the third quarter of last year, followed by 1.3% in the fourth. The contribution in the first quarter is just 0.95%.

Both gauges of inflation (CPI and PCE price index) are expected to rise in the coming months as higher energy prices and transportation expenses lift the rates. The longer energy prices remain elevated, the more likely they are to feed through to other goods and services. The CPI was at 3.3% in March, while average hourly earnings grew 3.5%. Accounting for the number of hours worked, inflation-adjusted (real) weekly take-home pay grew just 0.2% last month.

It seems inevitable to me that workers, who are also consumers, will see real wages decline during the second quarter. Tax refunds will soften the blow in April to a degree, but a decline in real wage growth has historically led to a deceleration in real consumer spending growth. This is bound to result in a very slow rate of economic growth in the second quarter. This is why every day that the Strait remains closed is another small blow to our economy. Aside from the technology sector, it should also weigh on earnings expectations, but analysts have yet to adjust their numbers.

Can the stock market look past these headwinds? I think we have seen the correction lows for the year, but I am very suspicious of any strength above recent highs until markets account for the temporary period of slower growth and higher inflation ahead. This certainly isn’t going to lead to an uptick in the labor market, as businesses that were contending with tariffs last year must now manage a sharp increase in input costs from energy, transportation, and other materials impacted by the disruption to global supply chains. Not only are food prices rising, but industrial metal prices are inching higher, with aluminum and copper up 10% since mid-March. This is probably why the latest NFIB small business survey showed capital investment plans at their lowest level since 2009. If this leads to a rise in unemployment, along with slower growth and an increasing inflation rate, we have a recipe for a period of stagflation. The Strait of Hormuz needs to reopen soon.

data.title: Mueller Industries Q1 Review: There May Be Short-Term Pain Ahead
data.description:
Mueller Industries (MLI) recently reported its Q1 numbers, which were received very well; however, I have some doubts regarding the short-term outlook, which can muddy the performance of any company involved in any way, shape, or form within the volatile mortgage industry due to the current conflict between the US and Iran.

By the Numbers

Looking at the top-line performance, net sales came in at $1.19B, up 19% y/y, a fantastic performance. If we look a little closer, we will see that the best performer of the bunch was Industrial Metals, growing to $321.3m, up 27.6%, followed by Piping Systems, with a growth of almost 19% y/y, coming in at $760.5m, and then Climate, which came in essentially flat, at $123.7m.

The revenue increase was mainly price-led. The management said that the improved sales across the industrial, electrical, and commercial markets stem from higher raw material costs. The company clearly benefited from a favorable combination of copper pricing and end-market strength, leading to a very good top-line performance.

Let’s take a look at the company’s latest margin profile. Q1 diluted EPS increased to $2.16 vs. $1.39 at the same time last year. Looking at the individual segments’ profitability, we can see that the Piping systems’ operating income increased to $217m, up 37.2%. Industrial metals increased 47.2% to $44.3m, and Climate disappoints yet again with a -6.3% change y/y to $33.4m. Overall operating margin came in at 26.2%, up 560bps from a year ago. At first glance, the improvements are fantastic in terms of profitability; however, if we look at the 8-K, we will see that this quarter, the company recorded a one-time gain on the sale of business for approximately $41.4m. Without it, operating margins still improved around 200bps, but not as impressively. The company sold its Sherwood Valve business.

Now, let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. MLI finished the first quarter with around $1.382B in cash and equivalents, against zero long-term debt, apart from some operating lease liabilities that don’t amount to anything serious. So, the company’s balance sheet is really strong. MLI has plenty of cash to deploy for growth initiatives or rewarding shareholders in one way or another, whether that is a dividend increase, which it already increased by 40% back in February, or share buybacks, which they are also actively doing, since it used most of the cash from operations to buy back 650,000 shares in the latest quarter for $75m.

Speaking of cash flow, cash from operations came in lower than last year at the same time, $79.8m vs. $113.5m, while capex spending remained close to the same rate it made last year. So overall, free cash flow was lower because CFO was lower.

In terms of guidance for Q2 or full-year 2026, the management didn’t give anything in terms of numbers, only in terms of qualitative outlook, saying that business conditions and their outlook remain consistent with the annual report and that they expect mix shifts, shipments, and production to improve over time, and they also see broader upside that is tied to the recovery in US residential and commercial construction and better conditions abroad.

It seems the reaction to the company’s results for the past quarter was received very well. On the day of the report, the company’s share price skyrocketed over 11% on the back of improved profitability, strong top-line growth, as well as the management’s comments regarding the outlook, which was a lot more upbeat than the management said back in Q4, when they were a lot more worried about a tougher near-term backdrop, with some offset from pricing and mix, but tariff uncertainty and construction softness could weigh on the year. However, it seemed that tariffs were actually a tailwind because end-market demand remained strong, even with elevated prices in place.

Mortgage recovery worries

Equifax (EFX) also recently reported its earnings, and they specifically said that they are worried about mortgage activity declining after being frontloaded in January and February. This is due to the escalation of the Iran conflict, which worries me slightly because MLI’s management said that they expect to see an improvement in the US residential and commercial construction markets, or at least they said they are looking forward to an improvement in these markets going forward. So, taking what Equifax management said at face value, we should expect to see the next quarter’s mortgage activity to be somewhat muted and even decline because of the conflict, which brought up interest rates on mortgages. Less activity means less need for building apartments, houses, and commercial real estate because most of the people will try to wait out the conflict. The conflict is meant to go away eventually, but with the back-and-forth of the two parties continuing to go nowhere productive, that means we could see a couple more quarters of disruptions, and that means less activity as well.

On the other hand, we could see that this impact may be limited and may not affect overall demand as much as I am thinking. Look at what tariffs did for the company. They were essentially a tailwind as the demand continued to be strong for the company, as enterprises were more than glad to absorb the increases in cost. They may have been thinking that the prices will get even worse in the future, so building out right now is the cheapest it will be for a while. The same can repeat going forward. Customers may continue to build out, thinking that this is as good as it gets for now, and they would be losing out on building their premises or houses if they wait until it all settles, which could be many quarters.

Should you buy MLI post-earnings rise?

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to see how much the company is worth. Below are the assumptions I used for my 5-year DCF model.

I think the top-line growth is good enough, and quite consistent with its average over the last few years, so I am fine with that. On profitability, it seems that analysts are modeling around 24.3% operating margins to get around $8.40 in EPS for 2026, so I anchored my assumptions to that number and naturally increased them over the model because companies usually improve their operations in the long term. For the rest of the model, I kept the numbers close to MLI’s historical averages. So, with these assumptions, we get the following projections.

For the model, I am also using 10% as my discount rate and 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. And with these projections, we see that the company’s intrinsic value is around $121 per share, meaning it would not be a good time to start a position.

But does that mean you should sell if you already own it? Not at all. The company is growing at quite attractive rates, but the growth I modeled doesn’t seem to support the current valuation, and I will only look to add closer to a $110 share price if it drops down there again. If not, I would be looking to add if the company’s operations end up being much more attractive than what I have modeled above. Given the current macro uncertainty, I am going to remain on the sidelines because it doesn’t seem to be worth it right now.

data.title: BINC: Simple, Effective Income ETF With A Well-Diversified Portfolio
data.description:
The iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC) has been one of my top income ETFs of these past few years due to the fund's extremely well-diversified holdings, above-average 5.8% yield and performance track-record, and below-average risk and volatility. There are several ETFs with these same characteristics, including the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY) and the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE), but BINC is the most diversified of the bunch and a particularly simple, effective choice. BINC is a strong investment opportunity and a buy.

As I've covered BINC several times before, I thought to make this article a bit different by focusing on the fund's advantages and disadvantages compared to the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (BND), the largest bond ETF in the market and an industry benchmark. Doing so should show quite clearly what sets BINC apart from the pack, and why it's a particularly effective investment. Let's have a look.

BINC - Greater Asset Diversification

BND is a broad bond ETF, with investments in most of the major bond sub-asset classes, including corporate bonds, treasuries, and MBS. It explicitly excludes non-investment-grade securities, presumably due to risk considerations. As these riskier securities account for only 15%–20% of the broader bond market, BND still seems like an adequate bond benchmark. Allocations are as follows:

BINC is also a broad bond ETF, but with a somewhat different approach. BINC invests in a broader array of fixed-income securities, including riskier ones, such as high-yield corporate bonds and emerging market securities, and niche ones, such as CLOs and ABS. It generally lacks treasuries, although the MBS and quality corporate bond investments are something of a replacement for these. Asset allocations are as follows:

BINC's portfolio is arguably broader than that of BND, as the former invests in more types of bonds and fixed-income securities than the latter. BINC's performance could also more materially differ from that of broader bond benchmarks, for the same reason.

BINC's greater asset diversification reduces portfolio risk, volatility, and the probability and magnitude of any potential losses due to issues with any one specific bond sub-asset class.

BINC's greater asset class diversification could also serve to smooth out returns throughout macro cycles and conditions, as at least some of the fund's investments should perform well regardless of the circumstances. As an example, the fund's quality investments should perform well during more traditional downturns, while the fund's CLOs should perform well during periods of rising rates.

With inception in mid-2023, the fund has yet to experience any meaningful macro or economic cycle from start to finish. I do believe that the above is true, although it's somewhat speculative.

BINC's performance since inception shows reasonably stable returns relative to BND, tentative evidence in favor of the above. Not enough data to really know for certain, but at least there is no evidence against.

Higher Dividend Yield

BINC's 5.8% dividend yield is quite a bit higher than BND's 3.9% figure. The difference in yield is due to some combination of higher credit risk, active management, and BINC's investments in more niche securities with above-average yields.

BINC's higher dividends should result in outperformance moving forward, which brings me to my next point.

Stronger Absolute and Risk-Adjusted Returns

BINC has outperformed BND since inception. Outperformance is significant and consistent, barring a couple small time periods, including these past three months. Results are as follows: the 3y mark corresponds to results since BINC's inception in May 2023:

BINC's outperformance was due to some combination of active management, a higher dividend yield, and greater credit risk. Active management arguably includes the fund's investments in more niche fixed-income assets, including CLOs and emerging market bonds. These tend to outperform and have likely played a role in BINC's outperformance.

In my opinion, BINC's risk-adjusted returns are also incredibly strong, although the data is mixed. Sharpe ratios are higher than those of BND, and Sortino ratios are technically too, but only recently.

On the other hand, the fund's performance track-record seems to imply much stronger risk-adjusted returns than above. BINC has achieved almost twice the returns of BND since inception, and these are arguably more, not less, stable.

In my opinion, issues of timing and time period selection explain the results above. Specifically, we only have Sharpe and Sortino ratios for the past twelve months, during which BINC has performed, well, not badly, but a bit worse than before. Longer-term figures should be stronger, at least based on the graph above.

BINC - Weaker Credit Quality

As mentioned previously, BND exclusively invests in investment-grade bonds, with BINC investing in both investment-grade and non-investment-grade. BINC's credit quality is thus weaker.

Credit quality for BND:

Credit quality for BINC:

BINC's weaker credit quality should result in higher losses during more traditional downturns and recessions. As mentioned previously, BINC has yet to experience an actual downturn or recession, although there have been a couple of periods of moderate market stress. BINC has somewhat underperformed expectations during these. As an example, BINC saw a 1.0% drawdown earlier in the year, due to the ongoing situation in the Middle East. The drawdown was closer to that of a high-yield bond index ETF than to BND.

I've covered BINC for several years now, and I do recall a couple other similar occurrences, although I can't recall the dates. As mentioned previously, results here are a bit worse than expected, as BINC is seeing similar losses to high-yield corporate bond benchmark ETFs in spite of the fund's greater credit quality. The issue here is that these occurrences barely qualify as periods of market stress, let alone a downturn or recession. Wouldn't over-index on these time periods, as results could easily be due to volatility, liquidity, or similar issues. Some of these losses are due to BINC's small premium to NAV narrowing earlier in the year. The movements here are tiny, but so are the differences in returns for these ETFs.

BINC - Lower Rate Risk

BINC focuses on shorter-term securities, with an average duration of 3.4 years.

BND, on the other hand, focuses on medium-term securities, with an average duration of 5.7 years.

BINC's lower duration reduces the fund's rate exposure, decreasing returns during periods of significant reductions in rates, and increasing returns (more accurately, reducing losses) during periods of significant rate hikes. A

Although the differences in duration are material, bond prices and returns are dependent on many other factors too, including investor sentiment and credit spreads. These other factors could easily outweigh the differences in duration, especially considering the fact that BND and BINC are far from identical ETFs. At the same time, Federal Reserve rates only have an indirect effect or impact on most securities, including most of the securities held by both BND and BINC.

Considering the above, BINC is not guaranteed to underperform during a period of declining rates, and vice versa. As an example, BINC has outperformed BND since mid-2024, a period of moderate rate cuts, albeit starting from a very high base.

BINC - Other Considerations

I've covered BINC several times in the past, most recently in late 2025. I'm consistently bullish due to BINC's strong fundamentals, diversified portfolio, and above-average dividend yield. Performance is generally above-average, with BINC outperforming index ETFs tracking most of the major bond sub-asset classes since my last article. Results are as follows:

BINC's outperformance was mostly due to the fund's below-average duration, as interest rates on most bonds have declined these past few months. BINC's underperformance relative to high-yield corporate bonds was due to credit spreads narrowing these past few months (BINC does have some hefty high-yield exposure, but much less than 100%). Do bear in mind that spreads have widened since the closure of the Strait of Hormuz but declined for months prior to that and are net down.

I expect BINC's fundamentals and overall performance to remain strong moving forward. BINC's diversified portfolio should endure regardless of economic and industry conditions, so its benefits—reduced risk, volatility, and some measure of stability throughout the macro cycle—should endure as well. Performance should be reasonably good too, on account of the fund's above-average dividend yield, and with the fund's past track-record as evidence.

As mentioned previously, I believe that BINC's diversification provides the fund with some amount of protection against market conditions or events, so I would not expect significant losses or underperformance regardless of conditions. Do bear in mind that this isn't the case for many investments, including long-term treasuries (significant losses if rates increase) and senior loans (sizable losses and dividend cuts during most recessions).

Conclusion

BINC's well-diversified holdings, above-average 5.8% yield, and below-average risk and volatility make the fund a buy.

data.title: USA Rare Earth: The Thesis Just Got So Much More Bullish
data.description:
Since My Last Coverage - What's New?

For 2026 my very first article was on USA Rare Earth (USAR), and I'm not one to be superstitious, but if my first article and recommendation can provide these types of returns, well then 2026 will be a great year for me and those who read my work. Since then the stock has climbed nearly 60%, heavily outperforming the S&P 500.

The appeal I saw and still see is that USAR seems closer to start generating revenues than a lot of other mining companies. Because it's instead of focusing on building a mine, it's instead working on having the factory part done, buying the feedstock and then start generating a revenue. Once profitable this will help accelerate the investments into the mine deposit which at the end creates a vertically integrated business model. As most know, this type of model often then carries higher operating margins. For a mining business and an unproven company this is crucial.

Perhaps the biggest news that has come out since the last time I covered USAR is the massive $2.8 billion cash deal to acquire Serra Verde. Why this is so important, not only for the size given, USAR has a market cap of below $5 billion, but more so because it's the only company really producing rare earth minerals in South America. This is the feedstock I was talking about before. USAR will successfully cover this part of the operations, making the long-term picture just so much clearer now. I was bullish in January and so far the company has lived up to this thesis very well, I see no reason changing my rating. Reiterating a Strong Buy for USAR.

A Quick Look At The Business

USAR will operate with a manufacturing facility and a mine deposit. Initially it will have to source the feedstock from other miners but once the mine is active part of its feedstock will be from its own operated assets.

For any new readers I'd like to quickly cover the business model. In my first coverage I talked about this extensively, but the gist of it is that USAR will be a producer of magnets. With the upstream part now covered following the acquisition announcement, USAR will have a constant supply of the 4 main REE:

  • Neodymium

  • Praseodymium

  • Dysprosium

  • Terbium

These are some of the most crucial minerals in the world right now and companies are making every effort possible to ensure they have a constant supply chain for their industries. The US for instance has been investing in US-based mining companies, like USAR, or signing substantial supply deals like with MP Materials (MP). The industry is in a completely new phase and as an analyst I find this to be one of the most interesting times (and lucrative) to cover mining companies.

So with the upstream part covered, the next is the midstream part, which is the "Less Common Metals" facility that USAR has in the UK. Here the oxides mined in South America are converted into refined magnet grade alloys. An important point here is that this means shipments go between South America and the UK, not via the US supposedly. The thing is that there are no tariff rates here. This supply chain doesn't get punished like car makers did for instance because they have operations set up in Mexico and sold back and forth to its US factories as well.

The part where we could potentially see tariffs rates is when the alloys here go to the last part of the business model, the downstream part. The magnet facility in Oklahoma. The final product is Neodymium magnets used partly in fighter jet products but also electric vehicles. With the US setting to increase their military budget by 42% compared to the existing one, and EV demand still in heavy demand, USAR is well positioned to benefit from both.

The New $2.8 Billion Cash Deal - What's Happening?

Okay, so the big news recently is obviously the acquisition of Serra Verde, the largest non-Chinese company mining for rare earth minerals. According to the investor presentation Serra would hold a near 50% market share of non-China oxide supply by 2027.

The location that Serra has in South America produces about 6,400 tpa, is an open pit strip mining type with an ionic clay. The expectation is that it will grow to over 10,000 tpa by late 2028 with a phase 2 targeting 6,400 tpa capacity growth. The consolidated gross implied value of what Serra produces right now based on current market prices is nearing $5 billion. Considering USAR is paying $2.8 billion, $300 million being cash and the rest from stock issuance I don't think it's that bad. Then because Serra is also building out capacity this figure should more than double to over $10 billion gross implied value.

USAR has an 81% ownership in the Round Top Deposit with the remaining stake controlled by Texas Mineral Resources Corp. It's leased through the Texas General Land Office with a 19-year lease running through September 2030. The site is supposedly the richest deposit in the US for rare earth minerals

Combined with Round Top in the US, USAR's total upstream would be 17,100 tpa. But then we need to look at the midstream capacity. As I mentioned earlier this is run with LCM, which was a previous acquisition that USAR made. Capacity right now is concentrated in the UK, but next year operations will also open up in Lacq, France. With 10,000 tpa, making it larger than the current 7,500 tpa in the Cheshire, UK location. Through various expansions USAR is targeting 27,500 tpa in the midstream department. Those who pay attention will notice that this figure is vastly higher than the upstream figure of 17,100 tpa and the downstream figure of 10,000 tpa.

This means USAR has a pretty massive secondary revenue stream of selling excess metals as opposed to only relying on magnet production. If magnet sales would ever slow down, USAR can shift over focus here which would likely be very cash flow rich seeing as end markets include US automakers and defense contractors which are scrambling to get their hands on these products.

As for the downstream part, only about 600 tpa is operational now but the target is as mentioned 10,000 tpa. The biggest risk prior to this acquisition was whether or not China would just cut off supply of REE before the Round Top deposit could open up. This would have left the Stillwater facility just sitting empty doing nothing but costing money. Now USAR has a closed loop instead, going from the top to bottom.

Did USAR Overpay for Serra Verde?

Even with what felt like a big price tag for a company like USAR, I actually don't think it overpaid that much at all. Compared to some of the other REE miners out there and their capacities, in comparison to what Serra Verde has I think it was a great and very strategic acquisition. On top of this, USAR also seems to have paid a discount, partly perhaps because of where Serra is operating. More often than not you'd see a premium price for companies in the US as opposed to South American companies.

Serra Verde Expected to Deliver $550-$650 million of Annualized Run-Rate EBITDA by the end of 20271; Combined Company Expected to Generate c.$1.8 billion of EBITDA in 2030

Considering the guidance that USAR put out with the announcement, it only really paid 4.66x FWD EBITDA multiple on the mid range. Combined USAR would now trade at 2.54x FWD 2030 EV/EBITDA, considering the EV is now $4.58 billion. Materials companies tend to trade at 8.53x FWD EV/EBITDA, leaving a pretty massive gap to the upside that the stock price could fill. Again, I think that the EBITDA is the most logical profit line to look at for a mining company, same as it is for a midstream company working with natural gas and oil for example. Both businesses have massive asset bases that depreciate. Because these have been paid for, accounting for this shows the true earnings of the model. And in the case of USAR it looks fantastic right now.

So to answer the question, did USAR over pay? No, it most definitely did not. At the time of purchase Serra held $1.8 billion in debt / equity capital. Given the 2027 run-rate in EBITDA that is a coverage ratio of 3x on the midpoint, again very solid. Even more so considering it has $3.2 billion in PF liquidity as well.

Since 2022 there has been serious dilution with the share count rising from 44.4 million to 73.6 million by 2024 year end.

There will be a lot of dilution though for shareholders as a result of the way USAR is financing this deal. In my last article I mentioned how the historical dilution rate is a risk worth considering. To finance the deal USAR is issuing about 126 million shares. The last 10-K filing shows the share count sitting at 98 million, up 66% YoY. So the share count would have to grow over 100% in order for this to all go through.

The next report for USAR is set to come out on May 7th, so not that long to go. I'd be looking at any figures for the share count and further comments on the deal. Loss from operations will of course still be deep in the negative, sitting at negative $59 million last year.

Some might be worried that USAR has a leveraged balance sheet given that it's still in development and in the pre-revenue phase leading up to this deal. But in actuality, the liabilities are only $191 million, with no large debt position. The cash position is very strong too at $359 million. Of course, a large chunk of this will be used to finance the acquisition, leaving $59 million on the balance sheet. With the figure I mentioned for the operating losses, that leaves about 12 months more before USAR would need to issue shares to "keep the lights on" so to speak.

This sounds a lot worse than it actually is. The deal is expected to close in Q3 this year, providing a solid revenue influx that should drastically improve the income statement for USAR. This again goes back to how it will now be able to organically fund its capacity expansion with cash generated from the mine deposit in South America.

What then further improves the thesis of buying is the safety-net that USAR now has. Having listened to an interview with the CEO of USAR and Serra Verde they mentioned how "upstream has little investment incentives" because of the low non-existent price floors. Historically China could just undercut making any investment outside the country no longer economical because the profits simply weren't there. With safety-nets now in place, USAR has effectively secured their bottom line.

Interestingly, the deal from the US government of $1.6 billion is not going towards paying for this deal, but that might have been obvious when the existing cash and stock issuance are the methods. The cash seems to be from previous dilution, as opposed to any government financial aid.

The Full Picture

Okay, so having now gone over USAR a second time I can confidently say that the thesis I laid out the first time still stands, and on even stronger legs this time. The deal to buy Serra Verde makes sense both financially, and importantly so, operationally. It adds the crucial feedstock supply ahead of the opening of Round Top in Texas. With midstream expansion underway as well the outlook for USAR and the stock is very positive in my opinion. There are risks like price fluctuation in commodities, but the price floors set in place help tackle this. Dilution will be common, but the growth will heavily outweigh this part.

Considering USAR now trades at only 2.54x 2030 EV/EBITDA the upside is enormous in the next 4 - 5 years should the stock be valued the same as the sector which is about 8.53x instead. I'm sticking with my Strong Buy for USAR. I'll admit, I added to my position after this news came out and have been doing so since my first article as well.

data.title: Pitney Bowes - Reviewing The Validity Of The Bounce
data.description:
In this article, I will be reviewing the development, the upside and the validity of the movement of Pitney Bowes (PBI). I reviewed this company a few months ago, in this article, found the company interesting, and rated it a "HOLD". I considered the company to be too unsafe to be a valid or high-conviction sort of investment, citing balance sheet issues and other factors.

The recent moves by the company in terms of share price goes against my own thesis and render it, currently at least, invalid. The outperformance has been significant, as you can see in the cited articles.

The outperformance that we've in fact seen since that article suggests to me that I missed something in my last piece, or the market has taken a position of exuberance based on too little information. My ambition in this article is to determine whether this was in fact exuberance, or whether I missed something of importance in my last piece.

Speaking in favor of the former, not the latter, my rating at the time of the article reflected a broader market view of caution for PBI. Despite current positivity for PBI from both Quant and SA analysts, Wall Street is currently still at "HOLD".

PBI has a generally attractive, or at least fundamentally safe set of operations - but it's too far to say that the company has been "outperforming" anything over the long term. For the 10-year period, PBI has severely underperformed the SPY, generating negative returns of 30%. The recent upswing has only been visible since a significant decline, which saw the company go to bottom-level valuations back in 2019. However, the valuation trend seems to currently be that the company's price at that time was too little, and the lows in 2023 and 2024 have now given way to significant upside in 2025-2026E.

So what changes are the company seeing that are causing some analysts, who have recently been neutral at much lower prices than today, to go from "HOLD" to "BUY"? Is this, in fact, a turning point for the business, and it's time to add this company to the portfolio?

Pitney Bowes for 1Q26 - an earnings preview

Part of the upside for PBI is the recently given 1Q26 earnings outlook upgrade. It not only raised forecasts, but also guided for improvements. However, at a first glance, the company's upgrades, while impressive, don't exactly denote a massive context change for the business.

We have a good increase in AEPS, and we have in fact a decline of 3% for revenues being forecasted. The primary reason for the upswing following this guidance seems to be a slight, sub-5% outperformance on the basis of revenues, as well as an adjusted EBIT outperformance of about 8 cents per share. FCF is now expected at $362.5 instead of $355. These are not result upgrades, or indications that I would consider the basis for a 40% upswing in a short time, unless they serve as a reflection for a much better underlying trend - something the market was pricing in (in opposition) before this guidance update.

It's objectively true that I made the wrong call on the business - but was this foreseeable?

I would argue that it was not - and even if it was, the upside there was extremely volatile and risky. The company's revenue declines for over a decade's worth of time as the company's legacy business models have declined, and financial issues caused by the GEC business segment. Going through filings, I argue that the company's turnaround, at least insofar as 1Q26 and potentially 2026 goes, was in fact hidden by persistent challenges on the financial side. The combination of 7-8% revenue decline, the drag from the GEC segment ($740M in losses in 2 recent years alone) masked profitability from other segments, and made it uncertain when any upside would be visible. I've seen some argue the buying signals caused by insider trading, but want to make it clear that net selling has been outpacing the purchases for some time. I consider it therefore not to be a clear sign.

I argue further, that investors who are entering now (following the outperformance - obviously those that entered before have seen a very substantial upside) are doing so in a potentially perilous situation.

I've been going back to the company's recent 10 years of filings to see what could have happened here. What I find is that despite share price increases, the company's revenues continue to decline. Guidance given by the company yesterday (Tuesday) in fact suggests a 4% revenue decline, which is lower than the 7% drop in 2025, but still a drop. Sendtech (meaning physical letters) is just as problematic as it has been in a declining legacy world due to lower installed base, and presort, while attractive, has not been performing well, seeing volume declines. The company was cited/commenting about these lost clients in 3Q25, for instance.

PBI, despite improvements, relies on the combination of high share buybacks, cost cuts, and layoffs rather than organic growth to see rallies in the share price. A similar trend has happened at least twice before, the last in 2019-2020, when the company broke its 5-6-year tradition of loss by managing a slight EPS increase in 2021, right before AEPS continued to crater.

While I am willing to concede that the company is definitely able to generate higher earnings than 4-15 cents per share, as was the bottom level a few years back, I argue that the few recent years of upswing are caused by non-recurring and non-organic trends, meaning the upswing is suspect. This is also confirmed by a more than 80% earnings forecast miss ratio.

The EPS growth you see is mostly cost-cutting, not growth. Reduction initiatives include exiting GEC, and it wasn't long ago than 2025, last year, that the company cut its own guidance. Despite the earnings you see, the company has over $2B in debt and interest payments are therefore considerable. The buybacks are further problematic, because the company has combined them with convertible notes, providing potential further dilution that could defeat the purpose. Current estimates do not support a growing top line base.

The EBITDA we saw in 2025 is according to the same estimates, the peak that we'll see. Anything above $560M is not in the cards from here on out, according this, and it would also not make sense given the company's EBITDA and operating margin to see the company extract even more value out of a shrinking revenue base.

Where the company has done very well is increasing its margins, really impressively delivering per share upside and growth. Current estimates for GAAP are $1.7/share by 2028-2029, which implies some further upside for those years. However, the problem that I have with that is that if you take the complete picture of the filings, the picture is not good. I find a significant share buyback very recently, over $370M of it. Based on this buyback alone, the impact based on SO was over $0.15/share, meaning that without the buybacks, the company's EPS would have been materially lower. The reduction in share count has been material for the company's updated EPS guidance - without it, the company would likely not be seeing this.

Bullish analysts have a tendency to see the bottom-line results as a positive indicator without looking too much deeper at what's causing it. To me, the logic of using hard-earned capital and what amounts to over half of the company's annualized FCF for a strictly financial engineering ambition, while the company continues to carry substantial debt and has a junk-rated credit leaves, is simply not there. I also want to make it clear that the company's debt is in fact increasing, not getting less.

So while I congratulate the investors who have made money here, my consideration for the company's 1Q26 is negative. I consider the company's quarterly results, and by extent full-year-results to be more engineered than reflecting an actual operational upside, something that flows from the company's own guidance for a revenue decline. For me to be positive on this company, i'd like some guidance on the organic operational side, which at this time is absent.

While the company may cite revenue strengths in sendtech, getting back some customers in presort, the "improving sales trends" of which the company speaks take second fiddle, in my view, to continued buybacks. I consider these somewhat ill-advised under the current financial status of the company. In fact, as FCF looks now, and as the company forecasts it, the FCF barely covers the company's 2025 share buybacks.

My valuation is therefore as follows.

Pitney Bowes - Removing the buybacks and moderating the upside means this one is still a "HOLD"

Despite solid valuation growth, I am therefore issuing a warning more than an upside. I believe that the company's current results, while absolutely seeing some form of slight recovery, are much more driven by buybacks and cost cuts than they are driven by anything organic.

This does not make things "bad", but it means they are by nature non-recurring, and the company has yet to issue guidance that addresses the declines in revenue. The problem with investing in a company declining in revenues and when any gains are coming from either a reduction of shares, or cost savings, is that eventually the company runs out of assets or money to do so. If the company had had less debt, it would have been a different story, but as of right now, PBI's annual interest expense consumes more than 25% of its annual FCF. The company has also shown no interest in reducing its leverage, favoring buybacks instead of putting money that could have been used to pay down nearly 4 years of interest expenses, to handling of debt.

It tells me that PBI is playing a quarterly, or short-term game. If there is any significant long-term strategy, to address nearly $2B in debt, I do not see it. The company's current net income trend for 2025 should not be viewed as a turnaround. First of all, it's a far cry from anywhere the company has been in terms of non-negative for before 2019, and secondly, it was based largely on the aforementioned cost-cutting and restructuring.

It's very positive that the company moved away from E-commerce. It means we can focus on what the company can actually generate from its sustainable operations. The problem I am having is that I don't see it clearly enough how much that would be.

Raising my PT, which was established based on exactly this sort of thesis, would require for me to see a scenario where I can put a continuing FCF at a sustainable level for the company, and a way for this B+ rated business to handle its debt. But remember that the combination of common dividends, buybacks of shares, and interest costs, consumes in excess of $150M of capital per year. Unless the company cuts SBC, cuts dividends, or addresses its debt, I believe this "train" is going only towards one direction - down.

I see no justification for increases and investing, unless you're a short-term investor, in which case I can see a short-term upside from further buybacks and other forms of financial adjustments.

But this is not how I invest - so I say "HOLD", and i'm staying away.

Risks & Considerations

Execution and growth risks are the primary issues with PBI. The valuation is attractive only from the perspective of historical metrics. I view any such metrics to be flawed because Pitney & Bowes is no longer the same company, either in terms of operations or in terms of balance sheet, that it was when those averages were "set". Thus, I say that even 10-12x P/E might be too high for this company. It trades lower than the sector median, but sector median companies include A-rated global shipping giants—PBI is a comparatively small, $1B market cap B+-rated company. Its profit margins do not change my risk assessment for the company, even if I can see the potential for returns being there, because many of the numbers are inflated by aggressive cost-cutting and buybacks, masking what the company's potential upside could be.

Beyond volumes of mail and beyond debt, the competition in this segment is not "asleep" - and I don't see as yet that the company has proven its long-term turnaround appeal. Consider also that the company already pays high interest for debt, and rising interest rates could further exacerbate this.

My thesis is therefore as follows.

Thesis

PBI is an interesting, tradition-laced company and a victim of a decline in legacy services, similar to Kodak or other companies. Covering these companies has taught me not to overestimate the turnaround potential of these legacy businesses and not overstate the appeal of legacy brands. I would therefore, based on fundamentals and risk, assign a very low conservative PT and FV to the company.

Because I consider the growth rates that are being estimated (12%+ per year) unlikely, also based on previous results and estimates for 2025, I reiterate my target for the company, a price target of about $8.5. Despite a guidance improvement for 1Q, I don't view this upside as material, given that it's mostly based on a continuation of the previous. In order for me to invest, I'd need to see better visibility of the eventual plan. The recent volatility does not make it more attractive to me.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which is not enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a price target of $8.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: RTX Corporation: Flying High Operationally, Grounded Temporarily By Expectations
data.description:
Introduction

RTX Corporation (RTX) reported its first-quarter 2026 results yesterday. The stock subsequently fell by a mid-single-digit percentage, raising the question of whether management reported disappointing results or whether this was simply the reaction of a previously overly optimistic market.

I've covered RTX several times here on Seeking Alpha, the last time a year ago following the release of the first-quarter 2025 earnings report (in which I also highlighted the previous years' performance). Back then, too, RTX shares fell in response to the report. In hindsight, the price decline following last year's earnings release represented an excellent buying opportunity. RTX continues—undoubtedly—to operate in an extremely healthy demand environment, both from the commercial aviation and defense sectors, so the strong stock performance appears entirely justified.

Against this backdrop, I'll provide an update of the company's latest performance and discuss whether the post-earnings price drop was justified from an operational perspective or was merely a case of a stock run hot letting off some steam.

RTX Corporation Q1 2026 Earnings: Solid Performance Throughout The Portfolio

For Q1 2026, RTX Corp. reported adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $1.78, up 21% year-over-year, including $0.27 of acquisition-related accounting adjustments. However, also on a GAAP basis, RTX's Q1 EPS grew 32% year-over-year.

Considering the dynamics underlying quarterly earnings, I think it makes much more sense to focus on top-line growth to assess RTX's quarterly performance. However, also in this regard, the company delivered once again a strong performance. GAAP sales came in at $22.1 billion, up 9% year-over-year, or 10% organically.

Management increased full-year guidance for both adjusted sales and adjusted EPS by 0.5% and 1.5%, respectively. Of note, management continues to report adjusted sales alongside GAAP sales, but in Q1 2026, no sales-related adjustments were made. However, since management left full-year organic growth guidance unchanged at 5% to 6%, investors should expect a slowdown from current levels as the year progresses. Nevertheless, RTX continues to operate in a very healthy demand environment, which is reflected in its strong order backlog and, of course, in analysts' longer-term sales growth expectations.

Regarding backlog (Figure 1), I was pleased to see continued (albeit somewhat slower) growth in both RTX's commercial and defense segments, up 0.6% and up 1.9%, respectively, on a linked basis. That said, year-over-year growth of 30% and 18%, respectively, says a lot about RTX's strong market position while at the same time justifying a closer look at backlog conversion.

Figure 2 shows RTX's total backlog as a percentage of the previous year's product sales. Evidently, sales visibility remains excellent, with backlog currently representing more than four years' worth of 2025 product sales. At the same time, the slight upward trend could point to manufacturing capacity constraints, although the stabilization since 2024 leads me not to read too much into this observation. It should also be noted that the chart begins in the fourth quarter of 2021. A year earlier, backlog conversion still stood at a whopping 4.6x prior year's product sales due to the pandemic-related lockdown measures and associated secondary effects.

All in all, it is certainly advisable to keep an eye on RTX's backlog conversion and that of its peers. However, given how well these companies have navigated the post-pandemic environment (especially the massive supply chain disruptions), I would argue that RTX's currently very high relative order backlog should be viewed as a positive sign overall.

It's also worth adding in the context of backlog conversion that RTX continues to generate healthy free cash flow. Q1 2026 free cash flow was up 65% year-over-year, partly due to lower capital expenditures but importantly also due to meaningful net cash inflow from the collection of receivables (+$1.8 billion vs. -$372 million in Q1 2025), partly offset by payables- and accruals-related cash outflows. Additions to inventories were in line with the first quarter of last year.

Leaving quarterly cash flows (and related working capital volatility) aside, the company generated $7.9 billion in free cash flow in 2025 and expects growth of 8% in 2026, based on midpoint guidance (which was reiterated yesterday). This was reassuring to see, as was the strong growth in operating cash flow in 2025.

On a side note, management calculates free cash flow as is normally the case, by subtracting capital expenditures from operating cash flow. Personally, I treat stock-based compensation as a cash expense ($400 to $500 million annually over the last three years), which serves as a rough approximation of share repurchases ultimately necessary to offset dilution from performance shares. Moreover, I normalize working capital movements via a three-year moving average and include the recurring increases in other intangible assets ($500 to $750 million annually over the last three years). As a result, the free cash flow underlying my valuation model is currently around $1 billion lower than the reported number.

Looking a little closer at RTX's operations instead of consolidated figures, sales growth was solid in all three segments (Figure 3), and we can clearly see that Pratt & Whitney continues to benefit from the ongoing strong demand in commercial aviation (aftermarket solutions in particular, up 19% year-over-year). Military-related sales (+7% year-over-year) and commercial original equipment-related sales (-1% year-over-year) reduced overall segment growth, albeit to still a very healthy 11% year-over-year.

I wouldn't read a sector-wide and demand-related weakness in commercial OE into the aforementioned number, as Collins Aerospace reported 15% related growth in Q1. As the growth was attributed to higher volume in narrow-body and wide-body platforms, it could well be that RTX is currently facing stronger competition in its commercial aircraft engines business. Finally, Raytheon benefitted from volume growth in Patriot/GEM-T interceptors and naval munitions, among others.

Regarding profitability (Figure 4), the skew towards Collins Aerospace illustrates the segment's superior profitability. That said, it was reassuring to see that all three segments reported more or less significant margin expansion compared to the prior year (see mini-table in Figure 4). Considering the ongoing solid deployment of Pratt & Whitney engines (most recently, especially F135 engines used in Lockheed Martin's F-35 jet), I expect a slow but steady margin expansion, as this segment is set to increasingly benefit from higher-margin engine service contracts.

Having not looked into RTX recently, I found it reassuring that net debt continues to trend in the right direction.

At the end of 2025, net debt was 15% lower compared to the prior year-end (Figure 5). At the end of Q1 2026, net debt was essentially unchanged at $30.6 billion, translating to a leverage ratio of 3.6x midpoint reported free cash flow guidance for 2026. RTX's trailing twelve months EBITDA of roughly $14.2 billion translates to a leverage ratio of 2.2x—both very acceptable numbers, in my view. Importantly, rating agency Moody's changed the outlook on RTX's Baa1 senior credit rating to positive from neutral back in February after the release of the Q4 and full-year 2025 results due to:

RTX's strong business profile and […] expectations that a favorable industry backdrop and robust backlog will drive earnings growth and continued improvements in credit metrics [as well as] the strong execution and diminishing risk relating to the remediation of Pratt & Whitney's power metal contamination on the Geared Turbofan engine (GTF), as well as the company's continued focus on debt reduction [...]

Finally, there was no announcement regarding the dividend yesterday. However, investors should not be concerned, as RTX typically announces its updated dividend after the release of the Q1 results in late April or early May (see timetable). Given that RTX paid out approximately $3.5 billion in dividends in 2025 (<50% of reported free cash flow), that leverage has improved notably from its peak at the end of 2023, and in light of the company's continued solid operating performance, I believe it is realistic to expect at least a mid-single-digit dividend increase.

Summary And Conclusion

RTX Corp., one of the world's leading defense and commercial aviation companies, delivered another solid quarter.

Both sales and earnings growth were very healthy, and the rebound in free cash flow was encouraging to see. While the (modest) increase in full-year sales and EPS guidance was certainly nice to see, investors should expect a slowdown from a top-line perspective as the year progresses.

All three segments grew meaningfully in Q1 2026, with Pratt & Whitney standing out in particular with 11% year-over-year growth. Operating profitability improved in all three segments as well, and while Pratt & Whitney remains the laggard from that perspective, investors should appreciate the fact that the segment will increasingly profit from high-margin service revenues.

Backlog grew only insignificantly compared to the prior quarter, but I'd argue that the comparison is tough. The year-over-year performance illustrates RTX's excellent position much better, in my view. RTX is currently sitting on a backlog worth more than four years of 2025 product sales. However, while at first sight this suggests manufacturing capacity constraints, a comparison with prior quarters tells me that RTX is simply operating in an extremely benign demand environment—importantly, both from a commercial aviation and a defense perspective.

In retrospect, RTX management did a great job buying back shares with borrowed money back in 2023 after the GTF engine issue had surfaced. In this context, I am pleased to see that management remains disciplined regarding debt paydown: net debt is down 18% compared to the 2023 high, and this is also one reason why rating agency Moody's recently changed the rating outlook to positive.

All in all, I feel extremely confident with my stake in RTX Corp. That said, the shares look fully valued to me, if not modestly overvalued, and this was likely also the main reason for yesterday's modest sell-off.

At some point, a stock can only go down, despite undoubtedly solid results. After all, RTX stock is still trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of over 28, a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 19, and a forward price-to-sales ratio of 2.8—so certainly somewhat lofty from a historical perspective. RTX's valuation is similarly demanding from a free cash flow perspective. According to my updated sensitivity analysis in Figure 6, RTX would need to grow its free cash flow by more than 5% annually in perpetuity to be fairly valued today, despite a cost of equity of just over 8%.

It is quite possible that we see some further (temporary) downside—especially given potential short-term speculation about a resolution in Iran—but at the same time, I do not consider the valuation to be unachievably ambitious, which would justify selling some or all of my shares. Therefore, I will simply continue to hold my position in RTX.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: The AI Boom Is Broadening, And That Matters
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last year, I did an article on Taiwan Semiconductors (TSM). And since then the company has had its share price increase by 60.63% as of writing this.

In my last piece, I said that TSMC was the factory behind it all. I think that line has become even more true than when I wrote about it. The difference now is that I am looking at a wider AI demand set that I did before. And this is because, back then I was mostly focused on how more enterprise AI adoption would translate into more GPUs, more HBMs and more data center deployments. I think that was right. But clearly not the full picture.

What I had not focused on enough is what happens when AI stops being a tool that simply answers prompts and starts acting like a system that plans, executes and coordinates work. I am saying this because I note that in the just recent 1Q 2026 earnings call, management said that the shift is from query mode to command and action mode as noted by the CEO, Che-Chia Wei;

...The shift from generative AI and the query mode to agentic AI and command and action mode is leading to another step-up in the amount of tokens being consumed. This is driving the need for more and more computation, which supports the robust demand for leading-edge silicon...

To me this matters a lot because more tokens usually mean more compute. And more compute means more silicon. And just to be clear, not just in one form. It means more accelerator demand, more memory related content, more high end packaging and more supporting compute around the AI stack.

This leads me to the heart of my thesis and why I am reiterating my Buy rating. TSM is no longer just the company that benefits when Nvidia (NVDA) ships another GPU cycle. I believe it is increasingly the company that benefits when the whole AI system becomes more complex. And when hyperscalers deploy more custom ASICs, TSM wins. When they need more HBM base dies. Again, TSM wins. Also, when advanced packaging capacity tightens, TSM wins again. And as agantic AI pushes more inference and orchestration demand into production environments, then the chip content broadens further. Ad this is why I think the AI demand story around TSM is bigger today that the market is fully pricing in. Now that I have discussed the reason why I am reiterating a Buy, let's try to understand what the business is already telling us.

Business Overview

1Q 2026 was a strong quarter for TSM across almost every important line item. Revenue came in at $35.9Bn, while gross margin was 66.2%. Operating margin came in at 58.1%. And more importantly, HPC accounted for 61% of revenue, while 7nm and below made up 74% of wafer revenue. This tells me that the parts of the portfolio linked to AI and high performance compute are not simply growing. They are actually dominating the mix.

Management's FWD commentary was even better in my view. Because TSM now expects full year 2026 revenue to grow by above 30% in $ terms. 2Q 2026 revenue is expected to be between $39Bn to $40.2Bn. I also note that TSM also highlighted that AI demand remains "extremely robust" and that customers and customers' customers mainly cloud service providers continue to send strong signals. To me this is TSM not guessing from one order cycle. It is hearing the same thing from both direct customers and hyperscalers funding AI buildout.

Growth Drivers

The growth drivers from here look clear to me. First is node demand. N2 has already entered high volume manufacturing and is ramping on bth smartphone and HPC/AI demand. On the other side, N3 is also getting a bigger global capacity plan than TSM would normally add to a mature leading node. Specifically because AI applications are pulling harder on capacity. TSM is also adding a new 3nm fab in Tainan while bringing 3nm into Arizona Fab 2 and planning 3nm in Japan Fab 2. I my view that is not how a company would behave if it thinks this is a one year fad.

Second is packaging. TSMC said that advanced packaging capacity is still very tight. And that it is working with OSAT partners while expanding its own capacity. I also note that its large size CoWoS remains the main supply approach today, while CoPoS is still under development. I am bringing this up because AI economics increasingly get trapped at packaging and integration. And not just at the front end wafer stage. This means that if TSM keeps winning both wafer and packaging dollars, then the company captures a larger share of the AI wallet per system shipped.

Third is ecosystem demand. According to IDC, AI infrastructure spending reached $318Bn in 2025. This is more than double in prior years. Additionally, Gartner sees semiconductor market crossing $1.3Tn in 2026. McKinsey & Company also found that AI use keeps widening with 88% of respondents saying that their organizations and companies ow regularly use AI in at least one function and 23% already scaling agentic AI somewhere in the enterprise. Now when I put those industry data points next to TSM's own guidance, then I see a company that is sitting where software ambition becomes silicon demand.

Valuation

Before moving to valuation multiples, I note that the TSM scores a B+Growth grade. TSM has its revenue growth rate y/y at 30.66% while the sector median stands at 9.73%. On the other side, the FWD revenue growth rate sits at 32.88% while the sector median sits at 10.81%. Additionally, I also note that the EPS FWD Long term growth stands at 31.44% while the sector median stands at 16.19%. This means that TSM expects to grow its EPS faster than the sector median.

Now, when I come to the valuation multiples I note that TSM scores a D valuation grade. TSM is trading at a FWD non-GAAP P/E of 23.70x while the sector median sits at 23.67x. This means that the company is trading almost in line with the sector. Additionally, TSM has its FWD EV/EBITDA at 13.39x while the sector median sits at 14.24x. But what actually caught my attention is the FWD non-GAAP PEG. Taiwan Semiconductors trades at a FWD Non-GAAP PEG of 0.75x while the sector median trades at 1.46x. This implies that the stock is trading at a 48.53% discount relative to the sector median.

When I compare TSM to its comparable peers, I note a couple of things. First of all Micron Technology (MU) trades at a FWD Non-GAAP PEG of 0.06x. This is quite low. It actually means that MU is trading at a 95% discount relative to the sector median. Then on the other side, Applied Materials (AMAT) is trading at a FWD non-GAAP PEG of 2.10x. This suggests a 43% premium relative to sector median. Next is Broadcom (AVGO) which has its FWD on-GAAP PEG at 0.85x implying a 42% discount relative to sector median. And lastly is Intel Corp (INTC) which is trading at a FWD non-GAAP PEG of 2.35x. Again a 60% premium similar to AMAT. To me, TSM still looks attractive at he current FWD PEG.

Key Risk

The biggest risk in my opinion is execution dilution from the very things powering the growth story. N2 ramp costs and overseas fab expansion will pressure gross margins. And management has already guided to 2% to 3% gross margin dilution from N2 in 2026 with overseas dilution broadening over time. The second risk is geopolitical and input cost volatility. I say this because TSMC mentioned that certain chemicals and gases could become more expensive because of the Middle East situation. Although it does not expect near term operational disruption due to multi source supply and safety stock.

Final Thoughts

Previously, I argued that TSM was the factory behind the AI boom. I would now add that the factory's addressable AI revenue pool is getting wider. I say this because Agentic AI means more tokens. More tokens means more compute. And more compute means more demand across accelerators, CPUs, packaging and advanced nodes. Yet, TSM sits right in the middle of that flow. For these reasons, I am reiterating TSMC with a Buy rating.

data.title: Energy Transfer: American Molecules Are Headed East
data.description:
Introduction

I always enjoy writing articles on midstream companies like Energy Transfer, (ET), even though they are "down the road a piece" from my usual Upstream core focus. There are a couple of reasons. First for dividend/distribution seekers they are a ticket to a predictable stream of income that can either be used for current expenses or reinvested into additional shares/units. Many members of The Daily Drilling Report are seeking just this sort of regular recurring revenue and appreciate the coverage. Second, they generate a lot of interest when they go over to the free side, as ET has a huge-175k following on Seeking Alpha. What can I say, writers love to be read.

I've covered ET many times, in my time here on this platform. Most recently with a buy last October. It's up about 10% since that time, but has still managed to underperform most other big cap members of the midstream cohort on a price improvement basis. A chronic feature of ET, but hope springs eternal...and then there is that still tasty distribution yield of 7.16%. It more than beats a sock in the jaw, I'd say. Please give that article a read for a general work up on ET, as I limiting this one to Q-4 status updates and focus on an aspect of the company that has only been lightly covered previously.

One of the things that brought ET to the top of the write-up hopper were posts showing tankers coming from the Middle East to the Gulf of America-they're ubiquitous-you've seen them, to load up with oil. It got me thinking about how this might impact U.S. crude and fuels exporters. The short answer. Perhaps only modestly over the short run as U.S. infrastructure is near physical limits, as noted by S&P Global at its recent CERAweek conference in Houston.

The US crude export system is approaching its physical capacity limits as a widening US discount to international benchmarks is driving increased flows to Asia following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, experts said March 23 at CERAWeek by S&P Global Energy. Latest data from Platts shows 38 crude fixtures booked between the USGC and Southeast Asia destinations in March so far, compared to just 13 in February.

But, ET has history of rising to meet the market with new infrastructure projects as we will see up the Q-4 update section. But first, let me review my American molecules thesis for those who haven't read my recent articles.

American molecules reign supreme

The Iran conflict has brought the vulnerability of shipping choke-points from the Middle East into sharp focus. As of today's writing, the Strait is closed as per Iran's military arm, the IRGC. U.S. has responded by blockading the country's ports, and attacking an Iranian container vessel that was attempting to run the gauntlet. As a result-and continuing the concerns shippers have had for their employees and the vessels they own, very few cargoes are making the transit of the Strait.
